paragraphs.text: Published by Bolan Society for Pure and Applied Biology 94 Research Article Optimizing wheat yield through seed rate and planting method interactions: A study for enhanced crop productivity and food security ShoaibZawar1 , MuhammadWaqas Yonas1,2* and MuhammadMujahid Akbar1 1. Department of Agronomy, MNS University of Agriculture Multan, Pakistan 2. College of Resources and Environment, Southwest University, Tiansheng Road Beibei, Chongqing-400715 P.R. China *Corresponding author’s email: its_yonas@outlook.com Citation Shoaib Zawar, Muhammad Waqas Yonas and Muhammad Mujahid Akbar. Optimizing wheat yield through seed rate and planting method interactions: A study for enhancedcrop productivity and food security. Pure and Applied Biology. Vol. 14, Issue 2, pp94-105. http://dx.doi.org/10.19045/bspab.2025.140011 Received: 03/02/2024 Revised: 29/06/2024 Accepted: 06/07/2024 Online First: 12/07/2024 Abstract Wheat (Triticum aestivum) is a vital cereal crop, contributing significantly to the global food supply. This study conducted between 2020-2021 and 2021-2022, aims to assess impact of different planting methods and seed rates on wheat productivity. Its goal is to optimize wheat yield sustainably by examining various factors affecting wheat production and related traits. It employs the Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD) with a double-factor split-plot arrangement. The study included three replications and evaluated three seeding methods: P1 (broadcast method), P2 (bed method), and P3 (drill method). Additionally, five seeding rates were considered: S1 (25 kg ha-1 ), S2 (50 kg ha-1 ), S3 (75 kg ha-1 ), S4 (100 kg ha-1 ) and S5 (125 kg ha-1 ).Thestudy indicatesthat seeding rates and planting methods significantly impact wheat yield parameters during both years. So, the drill method exhibited uttermost performance by using the seed rate 75 kg ha-1

during 2020-2022 using a seed rate of 75 kg ha-1 and drill planting method depicting 3135, 3033.3 kg ha-1 . It can be concluded that the integration of the P3 (drill method) with S3 (75 kg ha-1 ) has potential to enhance wheat productivity by improving crop standand resourceacquisition. Thus, thereis potentialto benefit both thefarming andscientific communities by enhancing food security, advancing agricultural knowledge, and supporting sustainable wheat production practices. Keywords: Food security; Productivity; Planting method; Seed rate; Triticum aestivum Introduction Wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) holds the distinction of being the world's most significant crop,surpassing all othercereals in both cultivation area and production volume. It is often referred to as the "King of Cereals." Notably, the wheat crop stands out as one of the most nutritive grains and plays a crucial role in feeding the global population [1]. Wheat reigns as the most nutritionally valuable cereal for protein, minerals, and vitamins. It accounts for over 60% of the daily protein requirements and contributes more calories to the global human diet than any other food crop [2]. Wheat holds a position of immenseZawar et al. 95 importance as a grain crop in Pakistan, cultivated on a massive scale each year. Although there is an increase in production, it occurs relatively gradually. Wheat's significance is underscored by its contribution of 1.9% to Pakistan's GDP and 9.6% to the value added in the agricultural sector. However, over the past several years, there has been a decrease in its yield. This decline can be attributed to outdated farming methods, inappropriate sowing techniques, suboptimal utilization of seed rates, a deficiency in farmer resources, utilization of lower-quality wheat seeds, and challenges in obtaining timely fertilization[3-5]. Thereisaclearnecessity to augment wheat yield through the utilization of optimal seed rates and sowing techniques [6]. The seeding rate stands as a crucial determinant for achieving the requisite plant densities, thereby fostering improved seed production. It profoundly influences both wheat yield and yield related characteristics, with the chosen sowing approach also exerting an impact on the seeding rate. Notably, the broadcast method demands a higher quantity of seeds compared to line sowing. Using a higher seeding rate results in more plants per unit area, which increases the competition within the crop. This interplay of factors significantly influences yield outcomes and production costs. Conversely, a reduced seeding rate bears the potential to considerably impact the final yield [7]. Sowing methods hold a pivotal role in wheat production. In developing countries, mostly a broadcasting approach is commonly adopted, inadvertently diminishing wheat yield. This, in turn, hampers the plants' ability to efficiently capture essential sunlight and resources, crucial for their growth [8]. The existing research highlights arid region wheat production challenges due to outdated methods, inappropriate sowing, and suboptimal seed rates. However, there's a gap in understanding how the interaction between seed rates and sowing techniques affects plant height, yield components, and yields in this context. Bridging this gap will provide insights into effective strategies for enhancing wheat production in arid zones, considering the agronomic practices under the light of resource limitations. The primary objectives of this study encompass the investigation of the optimal seed rate and the identification of the most effective method to achieve higher wheat yields, thereby enhancing global food security. The overarching aim is to bolster profitability per unit area, consequently elevating farmers' income. By addressing the challenges associated with seeding rates and sowing practices, the potential exists to significantly augment wheat yield through theutilizationofoptimal seedratesand best practices. Materials and Methods Experimental site A research study was conducted at the Muhammad Nawaz Sharif University of Agriculture in Multan, Pakistan. The university is situated in an arid region at coordinates 30°N latitude and 71°E longitude,with anelevationof122 m above sea level. Multan's prevailing climate is hot and dry, receiving approximately 175 mm ofrainfallannually.Thetemperatureranges from a maximum of 49°C during the summer to a minimum of 1°C in the winter [9]. Study period climatic data is shown in (Fig. 1 & 2). Experimental design, layout, and methodology The primary objective of this research was to identify the optimal seed rate and most effective sowing method to enhance wheat yield in this region. The experimental design employed a randomized complete block design (RCBD) with a double-factor split arrangement. The study included three replications and focused on evaluating three sowing methods: P1 (broadcast method), P2 (bed method), and P3 (drill method). Additionally, five different seeding rates were considered: S1 (25kgha 1 ), S2 (50 kg ha-1 ), S3 (75 kg ha-1 ), S4 (100 kg ha-1 ), and S5 (125 kg ha-1 ).Pure Appl. Biol.,14(2):94-105,June, 2025

96 The experiment utilized plot sizes of 7.0 m × 3.0 m, maintaining consistency across all plots. The main plots were designated for the various sowing methods, while the sub plots were allocated for the different seed rates. The chosen cultivar, Akbar-19, was planted in the second week of November during both years. The soil was appropriately prepared through ploughing and leveling, and the sowing technique involved manual broadcast seeding. All standard agronomic practices were upheld at their usual levels, excluding the experimental treatments. In the bed method, after manually distributing the seeds, the soil underwent meticulous preparation, resulting in the creation of beds using a bed planter as shown (Fig. 3). The spacing between these beds was adjusted to 75 cm. For the drill sowing method, well-prepared soil featured lines set 22.5 cm apart. Fertilizer nitrogen (N),phosphorus(P),andpotassium(K)was applied following the recommended rate of

. N and P were used as a base dose, while urea was applied in three separate applications. All other conventional agronomic techniques were maintained, deviating only due to the experimental treatments. Critical stage appropriate irrigations were administered. Figure 1: Temperature °C conditions of 2020-2021 and 2021-2022 wheat growing seasons Figure 2: Rainfall mm during 2020-2021 and 2021-2022 wheat growing seasons 0 2 4 6 8 10 12 14 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 11/1 11/15 11/29 12/13 12/27 1/10 1/24 2/7 2/21 3/6 3/20 4/3 Rainfall (mm) 2021-22 Rainfall (mm) 2020-21Zawar et al. 97 Figure 3: Planting plan of study Data collection and statistical analysis Harvesting of the crop occurred in mid April for both years. Data collection involved employing 1m-2 quadrant within each treatment to collect parameter samples. Parameters related to grain yield and yield components were meticulously recorded. Statistical analysis of the collected data was performed using Statistix 10 software. Analysis of variance was applied, and means separation was executed through Tukey’s Honestly Significant Difference (HSD) test at a significance level of 0.05%. The PCA biplot analysis was conducted by using Origin Lab 2021. Results Number of tillers Theanalysis ofthedatarevealedsignificant variations among the parameters of wheat yield when considering the interaction of different sowing methods (Table 1). In the year 2020-21, the highest number of tillers was observed in the P3S3 treatment, totaling 301.67, whereas the lowest count of 161.67 was recorded in the P1S5 treatment. Subsequently, in the following year, the P3S3 treatment demonstrated consistent and promising outcomes, yielding 295.67 tillers as the highest count, while the lowest count of 165 was once again observed under the P1S5 treatment. When assessing the data based on individual factors, it became evident that wheat planted using the P3 drill method displayed the maximum tillers values of 244.11 and 218.56 during both growing seasons, with the lowest counts of 197.78 and 183.11 observed under the P1 method, respectively (Table 2). Furthermore, the data indicated that wheat sown under the S3 method, coupled with a seed rate of 75 kg ha-1, yielded positive results in terms of tiller growth. Specifically, the highest tiller counts of 229.67 and 219.53 were observed under this condition. Conversely, the lowest counts were recorded under the S1 method during 2020-21, with values of 193.67 and 184.67. Plant height (cm) The study uncovered a range of impacts arising from the interaction between seed rates and sowing methods on plant height (Table 1). In 2020-21, the P3S3 treatment exhibited the highest plant height, reaching 97.04 cm, while the lowest measurement of 73.34 cm was recorded under P1S1. The treatment P3S4 treatment displayed promising outcomes, though they fell short of the satisfaction achieved with P3S3.Pure Appl. Biol.,14(2):94-105,June, 2025

98 Transitioning to the following year, plant height varied, with the tallest being 101.67 cm under P3S3, and the shortest at 70.67 cm under P1S1 treatment. Concurrently, the analysis of the data highlighted varying impacts of different seeding rates on plant height (Table 2). Specifically, the S3 condition demonstrated plant height ranging between 90.344 cm and 87.889, while the S1 condition had heights ranging from 81.122 cm to 77.778 cm during the 2020-21 periods. Moreover, they discerned the influence of sowing methods on plant height. In 2020-21, the plant height, measuring 88.70 cm, was observed under the P3 treatment, while the height reached 87.89 cm under P3S3. Conversely, the lowest heights were 81.75 cm under P1 and 77.78 cm under P1S1. Spike length (cm) When considering spike length, it becomes evident that the interaction of variables had discernible impacts (Table 1). The spike length exhibited variations and notable differences. Specifically, the highest length, measuring 15.24 cm and 16 cm, was observed within the P3S3 treatment across both years. In contrast, the shortest lengths were recorded at 9.61 cm in P1S1 and 8.67 cm in P3S5 during both seasons. The spike lengthwas notably influenced by thechoice of planting methods. The maximum spike length of 13.5 cm was achieved in the P3 treatment during 2020-21, followed by 11.90cmin2021-22.On theotherhand,the study identified the minimum length, measuring 11.09 cm in 2020-21 under P1 and 9.98 cm in 2021-22 under the P2 treatment.Furthermore, whenanalyzingthe spike length in terms of seed rate, positive outcomes were particularly notable with a length of 14 cm in 2020-21 and 11.83 cm in 2021-22. Although other seed rates displayed some encouraging results, they did not match the effectiveness demonstrated by the S3 method. Spikelet’s spike-1 The study examined the combined effects of seeding rates and sowing methods on the number of spikelets spike-1 (Table 1). The results demonstrated notable variations, with the highest counts of 18.00 and 20.00 observed under the P3S3 treatment for both seasons. Conversely, the lowest counts of 10.00 were recorded under P1S1 in 2020-21 and 8.00 under both P1S1 and P1S3 in 2021 22. Comparatively, other treatment outcomes were less favorable than those under P3S3. In terms of seeding rates, significant differences were evident in the number of spikelets spike-1 (Table 2). Analysis of the data revealed that the maximum count of 14.00 occurred under S3 for both seasons, while the lowest count of 11.00 was observed under S1 in 2020-21. Similarly, during the 2021-22 seasons, the lowest count of 9.00 was noted under S1. The performance of seeding rates based on this parameterwas as follows: S3,S1,S2,S4, and S5. Other seeding rate categories yielded unfavorable results. When evaluating planting methods in relation to the number of spikelets spike-1 , the study found that the highest counts of 12.00 were recorded under the P3 method for both seasons. Following P3, the counts were higher for P1 and P2. The research underscored the significant impact of planting methods on this parameter. The lowest counts observed were 11.00 and 9.00 during the respective growing years for both seasons. Grains spike-1 The grains spike-1 exhibited significant differences across treatment interactions. Within each treatment, considering the interplay of seeding rates and sowing methods, the findings revealed that P3S3 produced the highest grains spike-1 , measuring 56.00 and 54.34 for both growing seasons. In contrast, the lowest counts were observed under the P1S1 treatment, with values of 36.68 and 34.00 for both seasons. Other concerned treatments produced less profitable outcomes. In terms of the seeding rate, all treatments yielded unsatisfactory results, except for the 75 kg ha-1 . Under the S3 treatment, the maximum counts reached

99 Conversely, the minimum grain counts were noted as 38.78 and 37.89 under S1 for both seasons. The study's evaluation also illuminated the varied impacts of planting methods on grain spike-1 . Across both seasons, it was observed that the highest grains spike-1 was achieved under the P3 method, specifically measuring 46.20 and 45.00. Conversely, the other methods yielded less satisfactory results, with the lowest counts recorded as 40.68 and 39.80 under P1 for both seasons. This underscores the significant influence of distinct so planting methods and seeding rates on grains spike-1 . 1000 grain weight (g) The study revealed significant variations in the 100 concerning the combined effects of seeding rates and planting methods, as well as when considering each factor individually (Table 1 & 2). The highest 1000-grain weights of 47.00 g and 46.00 g were attained using the P3S3 treatment in both growing seasons. Conversely, the lowest weights of 30.00 g and 35.00 g were observedduringbothseasons.Furthermore, it was demonstrated that the maximum weight of 43.00 g and 42.00 g was recorded at a seed rate of 75 kg-1 across both years. Notably, the S3 (75 kg-1 ) yielded positive results, while the minimum weight was noted at 35.00 g in 2020 and 33.00 g in 2021. Intermsofplantingmethods,thedrill method exhibited highly favorable outcomes in relation to the 1000-grain weight parameter. The greatest weight of 42.00 g was measured using the P3 treatment in both seasons, with P3 outperforming P1 and P2. Conversely, the lowest weight of 37.00 g was recorded in 2020, and 36.00 g under P1 in the 2021 season. Grain yield (Kg ha-1) In (Table 1) grain yield data clearly highlights significant differences across all treatment variations. Notably, the P3S3 treatment exhibited positive outcomes in terms of yield Kg ha-1 . Specifically, the highest grain yield of 3560 kg ha-1 was attainedwith the P3S3 treatment in 2020-21, followed by 3430 kg ha-1 in 2021-22. Conversely, the lowest yield was recorded with the P1S1 treatment, measuring 2580.3 kgha-1 in both growing seasons. Amongthe different sowing methods, the drill method under P3 demonstrated superior performance, achieving the highest yield of 3135 kg ha-1

in 2021-22 (Table 2). Conversely, other methods yielded less productive results. In terms of seeding rates, the most satisfactory and maximal yield was achieved with S3, resulting in 3231 kg ha-1 and 3122 kg ha-1 in both respective seasons. Conversely, the lowest yields were observed with S1 and S5. Overall, the comprehensive analysis of the dataset consistently showcases the superiority of using a seed rate of 75 kg ha 1 along with the drill sowing method. These approaches consistently outperformed alternative methods across spike length, spikelet’s spike-1 , plant height, grains spike 1 , 1000 grains weight, and overall grain yield. The study underscores the critical importance of selecting appropriate sowing methods and optimal seeding rates to maximize crop productivity and yield. PCA analysis The Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was applied to the dataset in this study, resulting in the extraction of two components that collectively accounted for 73.64% and 82.32% of the total variation among treatments during both growing years 2020-2022 (Fig. 4). Throughout the study period, significant differences were observed in the responses of yield and yield components to the variations in the seed rate and planting methods treatments. Notably, theseed rate and planting methods were scattered across different quadrants, providing compelling evidence of substantial variations among the treatment levels in their association with improvement in wheat productivity. Among all treatments, the combination of P3S3 demonstrated dominance over the others during the course of the study to improve the productivity of wheat crop. Remarkably, all yield parameters exhibitedPure Appl. Biol.,14(2):94-105,June, 2025

100 a robust close association with the P3S3. On the other hand, the control treatments P1S1 showed negative associations with yield parameters and wheat yield. Discussion The analysis of the interaction between seed rate and planting methods for wheat cultivation yielded positive results in terms of wheat yield components and grain yield. This research demonstrated that the highest tiller height and productive tillers were observed when the crop seeds were appropriately placed at the right depth. After germination, each plant exhibited optimal plant geometry, allowing for specific spacing that facilitated healthy cropgrowth.Adequatespacingensuredthat plants could access necessary resources, leading to maximum plant height [10]. The number of productive tillers and tiller height were notably influenced by the seeding rate of 75 kg ha-1 . Using a higher number of seed rates per unit area led to a reduction in productive tillers m-2 . The drill planting method, which ensures uniform seed depth and spacing, resulted in more tillers. This is due to plants utilizing available resources more efficiently, resulting in better growth and development [11]. Moreover, the drill planting method had a significant impact on spike length, grains spike-1 , and 1000-grains weight. This method's uniform seed placement and spacing, in combination with the 75 kg ha-1 seed rate, led to improved crop establishmentandoptimalplantpopulation. When plants are well-established, they can better endure water stress during sensitive stages, as they all have equal access to available resources, allowing for efficient utilization. These findings are supported by the previous studies who highlighted the enhanced crop establishment and resource utilization achieved through the seed drill method, aiding in coping with heat stress during grain filling [12, 13]. Different planting methods and seeding rates also influenced the number of spikelet spike-1 and grain yield.Thedrill methodresultedin the highest spikelet count, attributed to its ability to establish an optimal and uniform tiller population. This, in turn, led toZawar et al. 101 maximum grain yield. The drill method's success can be attributed to its capacity to maintain soil moisture, conserve water, and enhance nutrient availability [14]. Utilizing a seed rate of 75 kg ha-1 demonstrated favorable outcomes for grain yield and spikelet count. Increasing the seed rate beyond threshold led to lodging issues in wheat crop. In this study during both growing seasons drill method and seed rate (75 kg ha-1 ) showed close association with all yield components which depicted that in this plot ensuring favorable environment for wheat crop (Fig. 4). These results are supportedbytheprevious findingsofwhich revealed that 75 kg ha-1 seed rate under proper plant geometry proved to be the most effective in preventing lodging, ensuring uniform crop stand, optimizing sunlight capture, maintaining normal photosynthesis rates, and facilitating efficient resource uptake by plants [15]. This study disclosed the interaction between seed rate and planting methods significant influence on wheat cultivation. The drill planting method, combined with a seed rate of 75 kg ha-1 , resulted in superior outcomes, including increased yield and yield components. This approach prevented lodging, promoted resource utilization efficiency, and facilitated better stress resilience. These findings emphasize the importance of precise planting strategies and optimal seed rates for maximizing wheat yield.Pure Appl. Biol.,14(2):94-105,June, 2025

104 Conclusion In conclusion, this research reveals the intricate relationship between seeding rates and planting methods in wheat cultivation, providing vital insights for crop productivity. The study highlights the effects of various seeding and planting approaches on yield and yield components. The best approach combines a seed rate of 75 kg ha-1 and drill sowing, offering precise placement that improves crop establishment, resource use, and stress resilience. This consistently outperforms othermethods, resulting in moreproductive tillers, spike length, 1000-grain weight, and higher yield. The study emphasizes the balance of seed rate, particularly kg ha-1 , preventing lodging while maximizing yield without compromising plant health. These findings guide farmers and researchers, underscoring optimal sowing methods and seed rates to unlock wheat cultivation's potential. This knowledge supports informed decisions for sustainable crop production, ensuring food security despite challenges. Authors’ contributions Conducted field trial, data compilation, and prepared first draft: S Zawar, Helped data compilation, data analysis, and final draft preparation: MW Yonas, Help in data curation, software, and first draft preparation: MM Akbar. Acknowledgments The authors wish to extend their appreciation to the Director of Farms MNS University of Agriculture Multan, Pakistan, for their generous provision of the required agricultural land for conducting the experiment, along with the accompanying agricultural inputs and resources.
